results.claim: Increased microtubule acetylation repairs LRRK2 Roc-COR domain mutation induced locomotor deficits.
results.explanation: The claim is well-supported by comprehensive evidence from a high-quality study (Doc ID 9638032) that demonstrates the complete mechanistic pathway. This study shows that LRRK2 Roc-COR domain mutations (R1441C, Y1699C) preferentially bind to deacetylated microtubules, causing axonal transport deficits and locomotor problems in both primary neurons and Drosophila models. Crucially, the study demonstrates that therapeutic interventions to increase microtubule acetylation—including deacetylase inhibitors (TSA), acetyltransferase (αTAT1), and genetic knockdown of deacetylases (HDAC6/Sirt2)—successfully rescue both the axonal transport defects and locomotor behavioral deficits. While the evidence base relies heavily on one primary study and uses model systems that may not fully translate to human disease, the mechanistic coherence and successful therapeutic rescue provide strong support for the claim.

evidence.passage: LRRK2 containing pathogenic Roc-COR domain mutations (R1441C, Y1699C) preferentially associates with deacetylated microtubules, and inhibits axonal transport in primary neurons and in Drosophila, causing locomotor deficits in vivo

In vitro, increasing microtubule acetylation using deacetylase inhibitors or the tubulin acetylase αTAT1 prevents association of mutant LRRK2 with microtubules, and the deacetylase inhibitor trichostatin A (TSA) restores axonal transport. In vivo knockdown of the deacetylases HDAC6 and Sirt2, or administration of TSA rescues both axonal transport and locomotor behavior.

Impaired axonal transport in motor neurons has been proposed as a mechanism for neuronal degeneration in motor neuron disease. Our results show that dysfunction of dynactin-mediated transport can lead to human motor neuron disease.

results.claim: Chlamydia trachomatis is most prevalent in the UK among individuals in their 50s and 60s.
results.explanation: The claim that Chlamydia trachomatis is most prevalent in the UK among individuals in their 50s and 60s is directly contradicted by the available evidence. The Natsal study data clearly shows that chlamydia prevalence is highest in the youngest age group studied (16-24 years), with rates of 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men, compared to overall prevalence of just 1.5% in women and 1.1% in men across the 16-44 age range. Critically, all available studies are limited to participants aged 16-44 years, providing no actual prevalence data for the 50-69 age group central to the claim. The claim fundamentally misrepresents established epidemiological patterns, as chlamydia is well-known to be most common in younger sexually active populations, not older adults.

evidence.passage: Chlamydia prevalence was 1·5% (95% CI 1·1-2·0) in women and 1·1% (0·7-1·6) in men. Prevalences in individuals aged 16-24 years were 3·1% (2·2-4·3) in women and 2·3% (1·5-3·4) in men.
Urine from a sample of participants aged 16-44 years who reported at least one sexual partner over the lifetime was tested for the presence of Chlamydia trachomatis

We analysed weighted data on chlamydia testing (past year), including location of most recent test, and diagnoses (past 5 years) from individuals aged 16-44 years reporting at least one sexual partner in the past year

results.claim: Aged patients are less susceptible to ischaemia/reperfusion injury.
results.explanation: The claim that aged patients are less susceptible to ischemia/reperfusion injury is contradicted by the available evidence. Clinical data from Doc ID 20606520 shows elderly patients have significantly higher 3-year mortality rates (57% vs 40%) in critical care settings, which would include ischemia-reperfusion events. The evidence on aging biology further contradicts the claim, with Doc ID 5035827 demonstrating that aging is characterized by chronic low-grade inflammation ("inflammaging"), which would typically increase rather than decrease susceptibility to ischemia-reperfusion injury. Additionally, metabolic evidence from Doc ID 39558597 shows that aging is associated with glutathione deficiency and impaired mitochondrial function, suggesting increased vulnerability rather than protection. No direct studies comparing ischemia-reperfusion injury susceptibility between age groups were found to support the claim.

evidence.passage: The cumulative 3-yr mortality rate among the elderly (57%) was higher (p < .05) than that among the controls (40%). The majority (66%) of the elderly nonsurvivors died within 1 month after intensive care discharge.

Human aging is characterized by a chronic, low-grade inflammation, and this phenomenon has been termed as "inflammaging." Inflammaging is a highly significant risk factor for both morbidity and mortality in the elderly people, as most if not all age-related diseases share an inflammatory pathogenesis.

Aging is associated with impaired fasted oxidation of nonesterified fatty acids (NEFA) suggesting a mitochondrial defect. Aging is also associated with deficiency of glutathione (GSH), an important mitochondrial antioxidant, and with insulin resistance.

results.claim: EG7 tumors can be killed by systemic oxaliplatin primed CD8+ T cells from the tumor-draining lymph node cells.
results.explanation: The evidence review reveals critical gaps that prevent adequate assessment of this specific claim. While the evidence supports general principles of CD8+ T cell activation in tumor-draining lymph nodes (Doc 27567994 shows CD169+ macrophages can crosspresent tumor antigens to CD8+ T cells), there is no direct evidence demonstrating that oxaliplatin specifically primes these cells. Similarly, although evidence confirms that T cells can demonstrate cytotoxic activity against tumor cells (Doc 15128866 shows CD4+ T cells can kill melanoma cells), no studies specifically test oxaliplatin-primed CD8+ T cells against EG7 tumors. The evidence consists primarily of mechanistic studies examining general T cell activation and cytotoxicity principles rather than controlled experiments testing the specific components of this claim. Without direct evidence linking oxaliplatin treatment to CD8+ T cell priming or demonstrating efficacy against EG7 tumors specifically, the claim cannot be adequately evaluated.

evidence.passage: CD169(+) macrophages phagocytose dead tumor cells transported via lymphatic flow and subsequently crosspresent tumor antigens to CD8(+) T cells. Subcutaneous immunization with irradiated tumor cells protects mice from syngenic tumor.
CD4+ T cell-mediated cytotoxicity eliminates primary tumor cells in metastatic melanoma through high MHC class II expression and can be enhanced by inhibitory receptor blockade
Efficient T cell activation requires an optimal dwell-time of interaction between the TCR and the pMHC complex

results.claim: Cardiac tissue-resident macrophages directly contribute to electrical activity.
results.explanation: The claim is well-supported by compelling mechanistic evidence from source 4928057, which demonstrates that cardiac macrophages directly contribute to electrical activity through multiple pathways. The study shows that macrophages express connexin 43 and form gap junctions with cardiomyocytes in the atrioventricular node, have negative resting membrane potentials that depolarize synchronously with cardiomyocytes, and modulate cardiomyocyte electrical properties by making their resting potential more positive and accelerating repolarization. Functional evidence includes photostimulation experiments showing improved conduction and depletion studies demonstrating that macrophage ablation or connexin 43 deletion causes conduction delays and AV block. While the evidence base relies heavily on a single high-quality study, the multiple complementary experimental approaches (anatomical, electrophysiological, functional manipulation, and computational modeling) provide strong mechanistic support for direct electrical contributions by cardiac macrophages.

evidence.passage: cardiac macrophages facilitate electrical conduction through the distal atrioventricular node, where conducting cells densely intersperse with elongated macrophages expressing connexin 43. When coupled to spontaneously beating cardiomyocytes via connexin-43-containing gap junctions, cardiac macrophages have a negative resting membrane potential and depolarize in synchrony with cardiomyocytes

macrophages render the resting membrane potential of cardiomyocytes more positive and, according to computational modeling, accelerate their repolarization. Photostimulation of channelrhodopsin-2-expressing macrophages improves atrioventricular conduction

conditional deletion of connexin 43 in macrophages and congenital lack of macrophages delay atrioventricular conduction. In the Cd11bDTR mouse, macrophage ablation induces progressive atrioventricular block

results.claim: Continued HHV-8 transmission among MSM in San Francisco may be explained by urogenital contact.
results.explanation: While there is solid evidence that HHV-8 transmission continues among MSM in San Francisco with stable prevalence rates (26-30% from 1978-1996), the evidence for urogenital contact as the specific transmission mechanism is insufficient. The available evidence primarily comes from studies of women showing associations with "risky sexual behavior" and past syphilis, but lacks direct demonstration of urogenital transmission routes among MSM populations. No studies directly link the persistent HHV-8 transmission in San Francisco MSM to any specific mechanism, including urogenital contact. The claim overstates what the current evidence can support about transmission mechanisms in this population.

evidence.passage: CONCLUSIONS Infection with KSHV was already highly prevalent in homosexual men when the HIV epidemic began in San Francisco, and its prevalence has been maintained at a nearly constant level. Any declines in the incidence of Kaposi sarcoma do not appear to be caused by a decline in KSHV transmission.

The prevalence of KSHV infection remained essentially unchanged between an MHS sample of 252 in 1984 and 1985 (29.6%) and a YMHS sample of 557 in 1995 and 1996 (26.4%), while HIV prevalence dropped from 49.5% of 825 in 1984 and 1985 (MHS) to 17.6% of 428 in 1992 and 1993 (YMHS)
In this cohort of North American women, HHV-8 infection was associated with HIV infection, drug use, and risky sexual behavior.

results.claim: Podocytes are motile and migrate in the presence of injury.
results.explanation: While there is compelling direct evidence from advanced multiphoton microscopy studies (Doc 123859) showing podocyte migration in injury models, the overall claim is not sufficiently supported. The evidence for baseline podocyte motile capabilities is entirely absent - all motility studies examined other cell types (melanoma, neurons, fibroblasts) rather than podocytes. Most evidence for injury-induced migration comes from general discussions of glomerular remodeling without direct demonstration of podocyte-specific responses. Critically, no studies directly compare podocyte motility before and after injury to establish that injury conditions actually trigger or enhance motility compared to baseline states. The strongest evidence shows podocytes can migrate in specific injury contexts, but this falls short of proving the broader claim about general injury-induced motility.

evidence.passage: Podocytes formed sporadic multicellular clusters after unilateral ureteral ligation and migrated into the parietal Bowman's capsule. The tracking of single cells in podocin-confetti mice featuring cell-specific expression of CFP, GFP, YFP or RFP revealed the simultaneous migration of multiple podocytes.

uPAR is required to activate αvβ3 integrin in podocytes, promoting cell motility and activation of the small GTPases Cdc42 and Rac1. Blockade of αvβ3 integrin reduces podocyte motility in vitro and lowers proteinuria in mice.

In animal models, regression of glomerular structural changes is associated with remodeling of the glomerular architecture. Regeneration of capillary segments might result from the contribution of resident cells, but progenitor cells of renal or extrarenal origin may also have a role.

results.claim: Having a main partner improves HIV outcomes.
results.explanation: The claim that having a main partner improves HIV outcomes is supported by consistent evidence from a large, well-designed prospective cohort study. The Swiss HIV Cohort Study (Doc ID: 17077004) with 3,736 participants demonstrated a statistically significant 21% reduction in progression to AIDS or death for HIV patients with stable partnerships (HR 0.79, 95% CI 0.63-0.98). While the evidence directly supports improved clinical outcomes, it lacks specific data on the proposed mechanisms such as medication adherence, viral suppression rates, healthcare engagement, and transmission risk behaviors. The evidence base is limited to primarily one high-quality study, which may limit generalizability, but the findings are robust and clinically meaningful.

evidence.passage: In an analysis stratified by previous antiretroviral therapy and clinical stage when starting HAART (US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention group A, B, or C), the adjusted hazard ratio for progression to AIDS or death was 0.79 (95% confidence interval 0.63 to 0.98) for participants with a stable partnership compared with those without
A stable partnership is associated with a slower rate of progression to AIDS or death in HIV infected patients receiving HAART.

During follow up 2985 (80%) participants reported a stable partnership on at least one occasion. When starting HAART, 52% (545/1042) of participants reported a stable partnership; after five years of follow up 46% (190/412) of participants reported a stable partnership.

results.claim: Combining phosphatidylinositide 3-kinase and MEK 1/2 inhibitors is effective at treating KRAS mutant tumors.
results.explanation: The claim is supported by consistent preclinical evidence demonstrating synergistic effects of PI3K and MEK inhibitor combinations in KRAS mutant tumors. Studies show that while PI3K inhibitors alone have limited efficacy against KRAS mutant cancers (Doc 4961038), combining them with MEK inhibitors produces "marked synergy" in tumor shrinkage in mouse models of KRAS mutant lung cancer. Additionally, synergistic cell death was observed with ERK/PI3K inhibitor combinations in KRAS mutant pancreatic cancer cell lines (Doc 4920376). The evidence suggests this combination addresses resistance mechanisms, as high basal AKT activity correlates with MEK inhibitor resistance, supporting the rationale for dual pathway inhibition. However, the evidence is primarily from preclinical studies with limited clinical data available.

evidence.passage: However, when NVP-BEZ235 was combined with a mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase (MEK) inhibitor, ARRY-142886, there was marked synergy in shrinking these Kras-mutant cancers. These in vivo studies suggest that inhibitors of the PI3K-mTOR pathway may be active in cancers with PIK3CA mutations and, when combined with MEK inhibitors, may effectively treat KRAS mutated lung cancers.

In contrast, mouse lung cancers driven by mutant Kras did not substantially respond to single-agent NVP-BEZ235. However, when NVP-BEZ235 was combined with a mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase (MEK) inhibitor, ARRY-142886, there was marked synergy in shrinking these Kras-mutant cancers

We determined that direct pharmacologic inhibition of ERK suppressed the growth of a subset of KRAS-mutant pancreatic cancer cell lines and that concurrent phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K) inhibition caused synergistic cell death.

A strong inverse correlation between Akt activity and PD0325901 sensitivity was observed. These results suggest that MEK inhibition may be useful therapeutically in tumors with V600E and non-V600E BRAF kinase domain mutations. The results also suggest that inhibition of both MEK and Akt signaling may be required in NSCLC tumors with high basal AKT activity.

results.claim: N-terminal cleavage increases success identifying transcription start sites.
results.explanation: The available evidence is insufficient to evaluate the claim that N-terminal cleavage increases success in identifying transcription start sites. None of the retrieved passages directly address N-terminal cleavage as a method for transcription start site identification, nor do they provide comparative data showing improved success rates. The evidence instead discusses unrelated topics such as poly(A) site regulation (Doc 17539488), sequence comparison algorithms (Doc 6128334), and N-terminal acetylation (Doc 8646760), all with low relevance scores (0.35-0.46). Without direct evidence linking N-terminal cleavage to transcription start site identification protocols or empirical studies demonstrating its effectiveness, the claim cannot be properly evaluated. A targeted literature search focusing specifically on transcription start site mapping methodologies would be needed to assess this claim adequately.

evidence.passage: Similarly, no consensus sequence was found when the sequences surrounding the 3'-ends of the longest transcripts were compared, but again weak putative 3'-end formation signals were identified. These data are suggestive of carbon source regulation of alternative poly(A) site choice in yeast.

To identify common principles of N-terminal acetylation, we analyzed the amino-terminal peptides from proteins extracted from Drosophila Kc167 cells. We detected more than 1,200 mature protein N-termini and could show that N-terminal acetylation occurs in insects with a similar frequency as in humans.

The error rate of all algorithms is greatly reduced by using statistical scores to evaluate matches rather than percentage identity or raw scores. The E-value statistical scores of SSEARCH and FASTA are reliable: the number of false positives found in our tests agrees well with the scores reported.

results.claim: Reduced responsiveness to interleukin-2 in regulatory T cells is associated with greater resistance to autoimmune diseases such as Type 1 Diabetes.
results.explanation: The claim is contradicted by the available evidence. Multiple studies demonstrate that reduced IL-2 responsiveness in regulatory T cells is associated with impaired Treg function and increased susceptibility to autoimmune diseases, not greater resistance as claimed. Doc 13923140 specifically shows that "reduced IL-2 production...correlates with reduced function of CD4+ CD25+ regulatory T cells" and causes autoimmunity. Doc 11899391 confirms that Type 1 diabetes patients have deficient Treg function linked to IL2RA variations that lower IL-2 signaling. The evidence consistently supports that proper IL-2 signaling is essential for Treg homeostasis and immune tolerance, making reduced responsiveness detrimental rather than protective. While the evidence quality is moderate, the direction of the relationship is clearly established across multiple independent studies.

evidence.passage: Reduced IL-2 production achieved by either genetic mechanism correlates with reduced function of CD4+ CD25+ regulatory T cells, which are critical for maintaining immune homeostasis.

Numerous reports have demonstrated that CD4(+)CD25(+) regulatory T cells (Tregs) from individuals with a range of human autoimmune diseases, including type 1 diabetes, are deficient in their ability to control autologous proinflammatory responses when compared with nondiseased, control individuals.

Immune tolerance and activation depend on precise control over the number and function of immunosuppressive Foxp3(+) regulatory T (T reg) cells, and the importance of IL-2 in maintaining tolerance and preventing autoimmunity is clear.

The development of Klrg1(+) Tregs also requires extensive IL-2R signaling. This activity represents a distinct function for IL-2, independent from its contribution to Treg homeostasis and competitive fitness.

results.claim: Tirasemtiv has no effect on cardiac muscle.
results.explanation: The available evidence is insufficient to evaluate whether tirasemtiv has no effect on cardiac muscle. While one study (Doc ID: 12631697) describes tirasemtiv as binding "selectively to the fast-skeletal-troponin complex," this represents the drug's intended design rather than experimental validation of cardiac selectivity. Critically, no direct studies testing tirasemtiv's effects on cardiac muscle tissue, cardiac contractility, or cardiac troponin binding were found. The evidence lacks clinical trial data evaluating cardiac safety parameters or comparative binding studies between skeletal and cardiac muscle. Without cardiac-specific experimental data, the claim cannot be properly supported or refuted.

evidence.passage: Binding selectively to the fast-skeletal-troponin complex, CK-2017357 slows the rate of calcium release from troponin C and sensitizes muscle to calcium

We developed a small-molecule fast-skeletal-troponin activator, CK-2017357, as a means to increase muscle strength by amplifying the response of muscle when neural input is otherwise diminished secondary to neuromuscular disease

results.claim: RANK-RANKL pathway signalling has no known association with development of Aire-expressing medullary thymic epithelial cells.
results.explanation: The claim is directly contradicted by strong experimental evidence from multiple independent studies. Research consistently demonstrates that RANK-RANKL pathway signaling is essential for the development and maturation of Aire-expressing medullary thymic epithelial cells (mTECs). Studies show that RANKL signals from CD4+3− cells and positively selected thymocytes promote the maturation of RANK-expressing mTEC progenitors into Aire+ mTECs, and that disruption of this pathway through RANK deficiency leads to impaired thymic medulla formation and autoimmunity. While the evidence shows functional relationships between RANK-RANKL signaling and Aire+ mTEC development, there is limited data on direct molecular interactions between pathway components and Aire regulatory networks.

evidence.passage: RANKL signals from this cell promote the maturation of RANK-expressing CD80−Aire− mTEC progenitors into CD80+Aire+ mTECs, and that transplantation of RANK-deficient thymic stroma into immunodeficient hosts induces autoimmunity

the cytokine RANK ligand (RANKL) was produced by positively selected thymocytes and regulated the cellularity of mTEC by interacting with RANK and osteoprotegerin. Forced expression of RANKL restored thymic medulla in mice lacking positive selection, whereas RANKL perturbation impaired medulla formation

Whereas only RANK signaling was essential for mTEC development during embryogenesis, in postnatal mice, cooperation between CD40 and RANK signals was required for mTEC development to successfully establish the medullary microenvironment

emergence of the first cohorts of Aire(+) mTECs at this key developmental stage, prior to αβ T cell repertoire selection, was jointly directed by Rankl(+) lymphoid tissue inducer cells and invariant Vγ5(+) dendritic epidermal T cell (DETC) progenitors

results.claim: HbA1c consistently overestimates glycemic control in black individuals with Sickle cell trait (SCT).
results.explanation: The claim is directly contradicted by the available evidence. The primary study (PMID: 26064662) from two large cohorts with 4,620 African American participants found that HbA1c levels are actually LOWER in individuals with sickle cell trait at any given glucose concentration, meaning HbA1c underestimates rather than overestimates glycemic control in SCT individuals. The study showed significantly lower prevalence of prediabetes (29.2% vs 48.6%) and diabetes (3.8% vs 7.3%) when using HbA1c criteria in SCT versus non-SCT groups, supporting systematic underestimation. While only one primary study was available and no evidence exists for alternative markers specifically in SCT populations, the available evidence clearly contradicts the claim's assertion about the direction of HbA1c bias.

evidence.passage: Among African Americans from 2 large, well-established cohorts, participants with SCT had lower levels of HbA1c at any given concentration of fasting or 2-hour glucose compared with participants without SCT

The prevalence of prediabetes and diabetes was statistically significantly lower among participants with SCT when defined using HbA1c values (29.2% vs 48.6% for prediabetes and 3.8% vs 7.3% for diabetes in 572 observations from participants with SCT and 6877 observations from participants without SCT; P<.001 for both comparisons)
These findings suggest that HbA1c may systematically underestimate past glycemia in black patients with SCT and may require further evaluation

results.claim: Cytosolic proteins bind to iron-responsive elements on mRNAs coding for TFRC1.
results.explanation: The available evidence is severely inadequate to evaluate the claim about cytosolic proteins binding to iron-responsive elements on TFRC1 mRNAs. The retrieved passages primarily focus on yeast RNA surveillance mechanisms (Doc ID: 16120395) and unrelated topics, with no direct evidence about TFRC1, iron-responsive elements (IREs), or cytosolic IRE-binding proteins like IRP1 or IRP2. While the yeast studies discuss iron homeostasis regulation, they do not address the specific molecular interactions claimed and may not be applicable to human transferrin receptor regulation. The evidence lacks specificity to any of the key components of the claim, making it impossible to determine whether cytosolic proteins actually bind to IREs on TFRC1 mRNA under physiological conditions.

evidence.passage: Tight regulation of the expression of mRNAs encoding iron uptake proteins is essential to control iron homeostasis and avoid intracellular iron toxicity. We show that many mRNAs encoding iron uptake or iron mobilization proteins are expressed in iron-replete conditions in the absence of the S. cerevisiae RNase III ortholog Rnt1p or of the nuclear exosome component Rrp6p.

RNase III-deficient cells are hypersensitive to high iron concentrations, suggesting that Rnt1p-mediated RNA surveillance is required to prevent iron toxicity. These results show that RNA surveillance through multiple ribonucleolytic pathways plays a role in iron homeostasis in yeast to avoid the potentially toxic effects of the expression of the iron starvation response in iron-replete conditions.

TNF expression of macrophages is under stringent translational control that depends on the p38 MAPK/MK2 pathway and the AU-rich element (ARE) in the TNF mRNA. Here, we elucidate the molecular mechanism of phosphorylation-regulated translation of TNF.

results.claim: Chenodeoxycholic acid treatment decreases brown adipose tissue activity.
results.explanation: The claim that chenodeoxycholic acid (CDCA) treatment decreases brown adipose tissue activity is directly contradicted by the available evidence. The primary study (Doc ID 1568684) explicitly demonstrates that CDCA treatment increased BAT activity in 12 healthy female subjects over 2 days, along with increased whole-body energy expenditure. In vitro studies from the same source show CDCA increased mitochondrial uncoupling and D2 expression in human brown adipocytes, indicating enhanced thermogenic activity rather than decreased activity. While the evidence base is limited to one small study with short duration, all available data consistently shows stimulatory rather than inhibitory effects of CDCA on brown adipose tissue.

evidence.passage: Treatment of 12 healthy female subjects with CDCA for 2 days resulted in increased BAT activity. Whole-body energy expenditure was also increased upon CDCA treatment.

In vitro treatment of primary human brown adipocytes derived with CDCA or specific TGR5 agonists increased mitochondrial uncoupling and D2 expression, an effect that was absent in human primary white adipocytes.

In rodents, BAT can be activated by bile acids, which activate type 2 iodothyronine deiodinase (D2) in BAT via the G-coupled protein receptor TGR5, resulting in increased oxygen consumption and energy expenditure.

results.claim: Stiff substrates encourage mesodermal differentiation by degrading beta-catenin in an integrin-dependent manner.
results.explanation: The claim is directly contradicted by the available evidence. Study 3874000 clearly demonstrates that soft (compliant) substrates, not stiff substrates, promote mesodermal differentiation through beta-catenin accumulation at cell-cell junctions. Conversely, stiff substrates inhibit differentiation by promoting beta-catenin degradation through integrin-dependent GSK3 and Src activity. Additionally, evidence from study 43661837 shows that beta-catenin stabilization (not degradation) is required for primitive streak and mesoderm specification, with up to 80% of cells developing into mesoderm progenitors when beta-catenin is stabilized. The claim appears to have the mechanistic relationship completely reversed.

evidence.passage: We found that, on a hydrogel-based compliant matrix, hESCs accumulate β-catenin at cell-cell adhesions and show enhanced Wnt-dependent mesoderm differentiation. By contrast, on a stiff hydrogel matrix, hESCs show elevated integrin-dependent GSK3 and Src activity that promotes β-catenin degradation and inhibits differentiation.

Here, we show that stabilized expression of beta-catenin perturbed human embryonic stem (hES)-cell self-renewal, such that up to 80% of the hES cells developed into the primitive streak (PS)/mesoderm progenitors, reminiscent of early mammalian embryogenesis.

We demonstrated that micropost rigidity impacts cell morphology, focal adhesions, cytoskeletal contractility and stem cell differentiation. Furthermore, early changes in cytoskeletal contractility predicted later stem cell fate decisions in single cells.

results.claim: Ethanol stress lowers the expression of PSP in bacteria.
results.explanation: The claim cannot be evaluated due to critical gaps in the available evidence. While the evidence confirms that ethanol exposure constitutes a stress condition for bacteria (Doc ID: 1148122), there is no direct evidence demonstrating PSP (phage shock protein) expression in bacterial cells under normal conditions or its response to ethanol treatment. The provided studies focus on other stress response proteins and regulatory networks but do not mention PSP specifically. Without baseline PSP expression data or ethanol-specific PSP regulation studies, it is impossible to determine whether ethanol stress affects PSP expression levels in bacteria.

evidence.passage: A module-level computational analysis was then used to reveal the organization of the contributing loci into cellular processes and regulatory pathways (e.g. osmoregulation and cell-wall biogenesis) whose modifications significantly affect ethanol tolerance. Strikingly, we discovered that a dominant component of adaptation involves metabolic rewiring that boosts intracellular ethanol degradation and assimilation.

Correlation of Spx-RNAP-binding sites with gene differential expression in wild-type and Δspx strains exposed or not to diamide revealed that 144 transcription units comprising 275 genes were potentially under direct Spx regulation. Our study globally characterized the Spx regulatory network, revealing its role in the basal expression of some genes and its complex interplay with other stress responses.

CONCLUSION These data indicate that at the early stage of interaction with host epithelial cells, a complex gene regulation and expression change occur in bacteria. Some of them might play an essential role during pathogen-host interactions and for the establishment of infection.

results.claim: miR-142-5P is a temperature-sensitive microRNA (miRNA).
results.explanation: The claim that miR-142-5P is a temperature-sensitive microRNA is well-supported by direct experimental evidence from source 6766459, which specifically identifies miR-142-5p as a "thermomiR" and demonstrates its temperature-dependent expression changes. The study shows that miR-142-5p expression increases when cells are exposed to fever-like temperatures (40°C) over a 24-hour time course, and that it participates in a negative feedback loop targeting immune genes including IL-6, IL6ST, TLR2, PGE2, and TNF. While the evidence lacks direct validation of miR-142-5P as a microRNA in the provided passages, the functional characterization and experimental demonstration of temperature sensitivity provide strong support for the core claim. The evidence is primarily from one high-quality study, which limits confidence but still provides substantial support for the temperature-sensitive properties of this miRNA.

evidence.passage: Reduced RBM3 expression resulted in increased expression of RBM3-targeted temperature-sensitive miRNAs, we termed thermomiRs. ThermomiRs such as miR-142-5p and miR-143 in turn target endogenous pyrogens including IL-6, IL6ST, TLR2, PGE2 and TNF to complete a negative feedback mechanism, which may be crucial to prevent pathological hyperthermia. Using normal PBMCs that were exogenously exposed to fever-like temperature (40 °C), we further demonstrate the trend by which decreased levels of RBM3 were associated with increased levels of miR-142-5p and miR-143 and vice versa over a 24 h time course.

We discovered that the expression of RNA-binding motif protein 3 (RBM3), known to respond to cold stress and to modulate microRNA (miRNA) expression, was reduced in 30 patients with fever, and in THP-1-derived macrophages maintained at a fever-like temperature (40 °C). Notably, RBM3 expression is reduced during fever whether or not infection is demonstrable. Reduced RBM3 expression resulted in increased expression of RBM3-targeted temperature-sensitive miRNAs, we termed thermomiRs

results.claim: Vitamin D deficiency effects the term of delivery.
results.explanation: The available evidence does not adequately support the claim that vitamin D deficiency affects the term of delivery. While the systematic review from source 2425364 demonstrates that vitamin D insufficiency is associated with various pregnancy complications including gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia, and small for gestational age infants, it specifically excludes preterm birth and gestational length from its outcomes of interest. The study's methodology explicitly lists "pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, bacterial vaginosis, caesarean section, small for gestational age infants, birth weight, birth length, and head circumference" as outcomes but notably omits any measures related to delivery timing or preterm birth. The other evidence sources are either irrelevant to vitamin D (focusing on antenatal steroids or lipid supplements) or do not address the relationship between vitamin D status and gestational length. Without direct evidence examining the association between vitamin D deficiency and delivery timing, there is insufficient evidence to support this specific claim.

evidence.passage: STUDY SELECTION Studies reporting on the association between serum 25-OHD levels during pregnancy and the outcomes of interest (pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, bacterial vaginosis, caesarean section, small for gestational age infants, birth weight, birth length, and head circumference).

CONCLUSION Vitamin D insufficiency is associated with an increased risk of gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia, and small for gestational age infants. Pregnant women with low 25-OHD levels had an increased risk of bacterial vaginosis and lower birth weight infants, but not delivery by caesarean section.

RESULTS 3357 studies were identified and reviewed for eligibility. 31 eligible studies were included in the final analysis. Insufficient serum levels of 25-OHD were associated with gestational diabetes (pooled odds ratio 1.49, 95% confidence interval 1.18 to 1.89), pre-eclampsia (1.79, 1.25 to 2.58), and small for gestational age infants (1.85, 1.52 to 2.26). Pregnant women with low serum 25-OHD levels had an increased risk of bacterial vaginosis and low birthweight infants but not delivery by caesarean section.

results.claim: Commelina yellow mottle virus' (ComYMV) genome consists of 7489 baise pairs.
results.explanation: The claim is well-supported by direct evidence from a peer-reviewed study that performed complete genome sequencing of Commelina yellow mottle virus. The primary source (PMID: 14376683) explicitly states that "the genome is 7489 bp in size" after determining the complete sequence of the virus genome. While there is a minor naming inconsistency between "yellow mottle" and "mellow mottle" in the literature, this appears to refer to the same virus species given the identical abbreviation (CoYMV) and genomic characteristics described. The evidence comes from a single comprehensive sequencing study, which is typical for initial genome characterizations of newly discovered viruses. The study provides detailed genomic analysis including open reading frame identification, supporting the reliability of the genome size determination.

evidence.passage: Analysis of the genome sequence indicates that the genome is 7489 bp in size and that the transcribed strand contains three open reading frames capable of encoding proteins of 23, 15 and 216 kd
We have characterized the viral transcript and determined the complete sequence of the genome of Commelina mellow mottle virus (CoYMV), a member of this group

results.claim: JMJD6 slows the hydroxylation of C-terminal lysine
results.explanation: The claim is directly contradicted by the available evidence. The primary evidence from PMID 37204802 demonstrates that JMJD6 acts as an α-ketoglutarate- and Fe(II)-dependent lysyl hydroxylase that catalyzes p53 hydroxylation, meaning it promotes rather than slows hydroxylation reactions. Furthermore, the study shows JMJD6 hydroxylates lysine 382 of p53, which is not a C-terminal lysine, contradicting the claim's specificity for C-terminal lysines. No evidence was found supporting that JMJD6 has an inhibitory effect on hydroxylation or that it specifically targets C-terminal lysine residues. The evidence consistently shows JMJD6 functions as an enzyme that facilitates hydroxylation, not one that slows it.

evidence.passage: JMJD6 acts as an α-ketoglutarate- and Fe(II)-dependent lysyl hydroxylase to catalyze p53 hydroxylation. We found that p53 indeed exists as a hydroxylated protein in vivo and that the hydroxylation occurs mainly on lysine 382 of p53.

Jumonji domain-containing 6 (JMJD6) is a member of the Jumonji C domain-containing family of proteins. Compared to other members of the family, the cellular activity of JMJD6 is still not clearly defined and its biological function is still largely unexplored.

results.claim: Activated Cdk5 phosphorylates the ATM protein in response to DNA damage.
results.explanation: The claim is well-supported by direct experimental evidence from PMID: 21295300, which demonstrates that activated Cdk5 directly phosphorylates ATM at Ser 794 in response to DNA damage in post-mitotic neurons. This phosphorylation precedes and is required for ATM autophosphorylation at Ser 1981, activating ATM kinase activity and regulating downstream targets p53 and H2AX. The evidence shows that Cdk5 activation by DNA damage serves as a critical signal to initiate the ATM response. However, the evidence base is narrow, relying heavily on a single primary source, and the mechanism appears specific to neuronal cells, which may limit its generalizability to other cell types.

evidence.passage: Here we show that Cdk5 (cyclin-dependent kinase 5), activated by DNA damage, directly phosphorylates ATM at Ser 794 in post-mitotic neurons. Phosphorylation at Ser 794 precedes, and is required for, ATM autophosphorylation at Ser 1981, and activates ATM kinase activity.
Thus, activation of Cdk5 by DNA damage serves as a critical signal to initiate the ATM response and regulate ATM-dependent cellular processes.

The Cdk5-ATM signal regulates phosphorylation and function of the ATM targets p53 and H2AX. Interruption of the Cdk5-ATM pathway attenuates DNA-damage-induced neuronal cell cycle re-entry and expression of the p53 targets PUMA and Bax, protecting neurons from death.

results.claim: Klf4 is important for proper myeloid cell differentiation.
results.explanation: The evidence supports KLF4's importance in myeloid cell differentiation, though with lineage-specific effects rather than universal myeloid influence. KLF4 knockout studies (Doc 25148216) demonstrate complete loss of inflammatory monocytes and reduced resident monocytes, establishing its essential role in monocytic lineages. Gain-of-function experiments show KLF4 promotes monocytic differentiation while blocking granulocytic differentiation in HL60 cells, indicating selective rather than general myeloid effects. While the evidence is strongest for monocyte/macrophage lineages and some passages lack direct KLF4 mention, the functional studies provide compelling evidence for KLF4's regulatory importance in specific myeloid cell differentiation pathways. The claim is supported but should be understood as reflecting lineage-specific rather than pan-myeloid effects.

evidence.passage: hematopoietic "KLF4(-/-) chimeras" generated by transplantation of KLF4(-/-) fetal livers cells into lethally irradiated wild-type mice completely lacked circulating inflammatory (CD115(+)Gr1(+)) monocytes, and had reduced numbers of resident (CD115(+)Gr1(-)) monocytes

inducible expression of KLF4 in the HL60 human acute myeloid leukemia cell line stimulated monocytic differentiation and enhanced 12-O-tetradecanoylphorbol 13-acetate induced macrophage differentiation, but blocked all-trans-retinoic acid induced granulocytic differentiation of HL60 cells

bone marrow monocytic cells from KLF4(-/-) chimeras expressed lower levels of key trafficking molecules and were more apoptotic. Thus, our in vivo loss-of-function studies demonstrate that KLF4, previously shown to mediate proinflammatory signaling in human macrophages in vitro, is essential for differentiation of mouse inflammatory monocytes

results.claim: The genomic aberrations found in matasteses are very similar to those found in the primary tumor.
results.explanation: The evidence base is too limited and contradictory to support the claim that genomic aberrations in metastases are "very similar" to primary tumors. While one mouse study (19736671) shows shared mutations between primary and metastatic skin cancers supporting parallel evolution, this single study cannot establish broad similarity across cancer types. Critically, evidence from intratumor heterogeneity studies (4489217) reveals that 63-69% of mutations are not detectable across tumor regions, directly contradicting the "very similar" assertion. The available evidence lacks quantitative measures of genomic similarity and comprehensive coverage across different cancer types needed to validate such a strong claim.

evidence.passage: Here we have used whole-exome sequencing of 103 matched benign, malignant and metastatic skin tumors from genetically heterogeneous mice to demonstrate that most metastases disseminate synchronously from the primary tumor, supporting parallel rather than linear evolution as the predominant model of metastasis. Shared mutations between primary carcinomas and their matched metastases have the distinct A-to-T signature of the initiating carcinogen dimethylbenzanthracene, but non-shared mutations are primarily G-to-T, a signature associated with oxidative stress

Phylogenetic reconstruction revealed branched evolutionary tumor growth, with 63 to 69% of all somatic mutations not detectable across every tumor region. Intratumor heterogeneity was observed for a mutation within an autoinhibitory domain of the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) kinase, correlating with S6 and 4EBP phosphorylation in vivo and constitutive activation of mTOR kinase activity in vitro.

Gene-expression signatures of good and poor prognosis were detected in different regions of the same tumor. Allelic composition and ploidy profiling analysis revealed extensive intratumor heterogeneity, with 26 of 30 tumor samples from four tumors harboring divergent allelic-imbalance profiles and with ploidy heterogeneity in two of four tumors.

results.claim: Toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling is involved in the pathogenesis of human MDS.
results.explanation: The evidence does not adequately support the claim that TLR signaling is involved in MDS pathogenesis. While one study (Doc ID: 3863543) identifies p53-S100A8/9-TLR inflammatory signaling as a mechanism in pre-leukemic conditions including MDS, this represents limited direct evidence. Most evidence focuses on related inflammatory pathways (NLRP3 inflammasome, S100A9 alarmin) rather than TLR signaling specifically. Importantly, evidence contradicts key aspects of the claim, with one study (Doc ID: 39851630) explicitly stating that MDS patient mesenchymal stem cells "did not show any aberrations in the production of proinflammatory or growth-promoting cytokines." The available evidence lacks direct measurements of TLR expression levels or TLR pathway activity specifically in MDS patients, making it insufficient to establish TLR signaling as a pathogenic mechanism.

evidence.passage: Massive parallel RNA sequencing of highly purified mesenchymal cells in the SDS mouse model and a range of human pre-leukemic syndromes identified p53-S100A8/9-TLR inflammatory signaling as a common driving mechanism of genotoxic stress

Despite genetic heterogeneity, myelodysplastic syndromes (MDSs) share features of cytological dysplasia and ineffective hematopoiesis. We report that a hallmark of MDSs is activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome, which drives clonal expansion and pyroptotic cell death.
Patient MSCs did not show any aberrations in the production of proinflammatory or growth-promoting cytokines and did not harbor the cytogenetic abnormalities present in hematopoietic cells.

results.claim: Inhibiting focal adhesion formation enables cells to convert mechanical strain into eventual scarring.
results.explanation: The claim is directly contradicted by the available evidence. While the evidence confirms that focal adhesions can be inhibited and that mechanical strain can lead to scarring, it demonstrates the opposite relationship than claimed. Studies show that inhibiting focal adhesion kinase (FAK) actually reduces scar formation rather than enabling it - FAK knockout mice had "substantially less inflammation and fibrosis" and small-molecule FAK inhibition "reduces scar formation in vivo." The evidence indicates that FAK is required for the mechanotransduction pathway that converts mechanical force into fibrosis through inflammatory signaling. Therefore, inhibiting focal adhesions would impair, not enable, the conversion of mechanical strain to scarring.

evidence.passage: Fibroblast-specific FAK knockout mice have substantially less inflammation and fibrosis than control mice in a model of hypertrophic scar formation

Small-molecule inhibition of FAK blocks these effects in human cells and reduces scar formation in vivo through attenuated MCP-1 signaling and inflammatory cell recruitment. These findings collectively indicate that physical force regulates fibrosis through inflammatory FAK–ERK–MCP-1 pathways and that molecular strategies targeting FAK can effectively uncouple mechanical force from pathologic scar formation.

We show that FAK acts through extracellular-related kinase (ERK) to mechanically trigger the secretion of monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP-1, also known as CCL2), a potent chemokine that is linked to human fibrotic disorders. Similarly, MCP-1 knockout mice form minimal scars, indicating that inflammatory chemokine pathways are a major mechanism by which FAK mechanotransduction induces fibrosis.

results.claim: Blocking the interaction between TDP-43 and respiratory complex I proteins ND3 and ND6 leads to increased TDP-43-induced neuronal loss.
results.explanation: The claim is directly contradicted by the primary evidence source. While the evidence confirms that TDP-43 interacts with ND3/ND6 mRNAs and causes neuronal loss, the key study (16966326) explicitly states that "suppression of TDP-43 mitochondrial localization abolishes WT and mutant TDP-43-induced mitochondrial dysfunction and neuronal loss." This demonstrates that blocking TDP-43's mitochondrial interactions actually reduces neuronal loss, not increases it as claimed. Additionally, no evidence directly demonstrates experimental blocking of specific TDP-43-ND3/ND6 protein interactions - only broader suppression of TDP-43 mitochondrial localization. The evidence base is also limited, relying heavily on a single primary source without independent replication.

evidence.passage: The suppression of TDP-43 mitochondrial localization abolishes WT and mutant TDP-43-induced mitochondrial dysfunction and neuronal loss, and improves phenotypes of transgenic mutant TDP-43 mice.

In mitochondria, wild-type (WT) and mutant TDP-43 preferentially bind mitochondria-transcribed messenger RNAs (mRNAs) encoding respiratory complex I subunits ND3 and ND6, impair their expression and specifically cause complex I disassembly.

Disease-associated mutations increase TDP-43 mitochondrial localization. In mitochondria, wild-type (WT) and mutant TDP-43 preferentially bind mitochondria-transcribed messenger RNAs (mRNAs) encoding respiratory complex I subunits ND3 and ND6

results.claim: ITAM phosphorylation prevents the transfer of the T cell receptor (TCR) signal from the echo-domain to the cytoplasmic tail of the T cell receptor (TCR).
results.explanation: The claim is directly contradicted by the available evidence. Study 8903143 demonstrates that ITAM phosphorylation is required for proper TCR signaling, showing that "dissociation requires phosphorylation of the TCR ζ immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motifs" and that this phosphorylation facilitates rather than prevents signal transmission. Additionally, no evidence supports the existence of an "echo-domain" in TCR structure - the literature describes extracellular ligand-binding domains and cytoplasmic tails but not this specific terminology. The evidence consistently shows ITAM phosphorylation as a positive regulatory mechanism essential for TCR signal transduction, not an inhibitory one. The claim fundamentally misrepresents the role of ITAM phosphorylation in TCR signaling.

evidence.passage: This dissociation requires phosphorylation of the TCR ζ immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motifs by lymphocyte cell-specific protein tyrosine kinase (Lck) but not ζ-chain-associated protein kinase 70 binding. Mutations of the TCR ζ BRS motifs that disrupt this membrane association attenuate proximal and distal responses induced by TCR engagement.
This study reveals that tyrosine phosphorylation of the TCR ζ cytoplasmic domain regulates its association with the plasma membrane and highlights the functional importance of TCR ζ BRS motifs.

The T cell receptor complex (TCR-CD3) is composed of TCR alpha/beta ligand binding subunits bound to the CD3 subunits responsible for signal transduction. We present strong evidence that ligand engagement of TCR-CD3 induces a conformational change that exposes a proline-rich sequence in CD3 epsilon and results in recruitment of the adaptor protein Nck.

results.claim: Treatment with the EC uptake inhibitor AM404 resulted in a dose-dependent decrease in the expression of immobility.
results.explanation: While AM404 is well-established as an endocannabinoid uptake inhibitor that enhances anandamide signaling (Doc 40476126), there is no direct evidence supporting its effects on immobility behavior. The evidence review reveals significant gaps: no studies examined AM404's impact on immobility, and no dose-response studies of AM404 on any behavioral outcomes were identified. The behavioral evidence consists entirely of studies on different compounds (propranolol, CB1 receptors) and unrelated measures, making it impossible to support the specific claim about AM404's dose-dependent effects on immobility expression. The claim appears to overstate what the available evidence demonstrates.

evidence.passage: Although AM404 did not activate cannabinoid receptors or inhibit anandamide hydrolysis, it enhanced receptor-mediated anandamide responses in vitro and in vivo. The data indicate that carrier-mediated transport may be essential for termination of the biological effects of anandamide, and may represent a potential drug target.

CD-1 mice were exposed to one, three, or six cycles of SDR and HPLC analysis of the plasma and spleen revealed an increase in catecholamines. After six cycles of SDR the open field test was used to measure behaviors characteristic of anxiety and indicated that the social defeat induced increase in anxiety-like behavior was blocked by pre-treatment with the β-adrenergic antagonist propranolol.

Further, in stressed animals both the low and the high doses of HU-210 induced anxiogenic responses. These findings suggest that chronic stress enhances either cannabinoid receptor responsivity or one of the interacting systems implicated in emotional states.

results.claim: Depletion of T-helper 17 (Th17) cells during simian immunodeficiency virus (SIV) infection decreases dissemination of Salmonella Typhimurium from the gut.
results.explanation: The claim fundamentally contradicts the available evidence regarding the relationship between Th17 depletion and Salmonella dissemination. While the evidence strongly confirms that SIV infection depletes Th17 cells in the gut mucosa, it consistently demonstrates that this depletion INCREASES rather than decreases Salmonella Typhimurium dissemination from the gut. The primary study (Doc ID 29023309) using rhesus macaques clearly shows that Th17 cells normally function as protective barriers that prevent systemic microbial dissemination through IL-17 and IL-22 signaling pathways. Supporting evidence from IL-17 receptor-deficient mice confirms that loss of the IL-17 axis leads to increased, not decreased, bacterial spread from the intestinal tract.

evidence.passage: TH17 cells were markedly depleted in SIV-infected rhesus macaques, resulting in blunted TH17 responses to S. typhimurium infection and increased bacterial dissemination. IL-17 receptor–deficient mice showed increased systemic dissemination of S. typhimurium from the gut, suggesting that IL-17 deficiency causes defects in mucosal barrier function.

Here we show that simian immunodeficiency virus (SIV) infection results in depletion of T helper type 17 (TH17) cells in the ileal mucosa of rhesus macaques, thereby impairing mucosal barrier functions to S. typhimurium dissemination.
We conclude that SIV infection impairs the IL-17 axis, an arm of the mucosal immune response preventing systemic microbial dissemination from the gastrointestinal tract.

results.claim: GATA3 regulates cell cycle progression in bone marrow hematopoietic stem cells.
results.explanation: The claim that GATA3 regulates cell cycle progression in bone marrow hematopoietic stem cells is well-supported by the available evidence. The primary study (PMID: 10165258) provides direct experimental evidence showing that GATA3-null mutant mice generate fewer long-term repopulating HSCs and that these mutant HSCs have reduced cell cycle entry rates. Critically, GATA3 mutant HSCs fail to increase cycling after myelosuppressive treatment with 5-fluorouracil, demonstrating GATA3's requirement for cell cycle entry in response to physiological stress. While the mechanistic details of how GATA3 directly interacts with cell cycle machinery remain unclear, the functional evidence clearly establishes GATA3's regulatory role in HSC cell cycle progression. The evidence base, though limited in scope, provides consistent and direct support for the core claim.

evidence.passage: In the present study, we show that Gata3-null mutant mice generate fewer LT-HSCs and that fewer Gata3-null LT-HSCs are in cycle. Furthermore, Gata3 mutant hematopoietic progenitor cells fail to be recruited into an increased cycling state after 5-fluorouracil-induced myelosuppression. Therefore, GATA-3 is required for the maintenance of a normal number of LT-HSCs and for their entry into the cell cycle.

Maintaining hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) quiescence is a critical property for the life-long generation of blood cells. Approximately 75% of cells in a highly enriched long-term repopulating HSC (LT-HSC) pool (Lin(-)Sca1(+)c-Kit(hi)CD150(+)CD48(-)) are quiescent, with only a small percentage of the LT-HSCs in cycle. Transcription factor GATA-3 is known to be vital for the development of T cells at multiple stages in the thymus and for Th2 differentiation in the peripheral organs. Although it is well documented that GATA-3 is expressed in HSCs, a role for GATA-3 in any prethymic progenitor cell has not been established.

Mouse bone marrow hematopoietic stem cells were isolated with the use of a variety of phenotypic markers. These cells can proliferate and differentiate with approximately unit efficiency into myelomonocytic cells, B cells, or T cells. Thirty of these cells are sufficient to save 50 percent of lethally irradiated mice, and to reconstitute all blood cell types in the survivors.

results.claim: Ectopic expression of Sall4, Nanog, Esrrb, and Lin28 generates induced pluripotent stem cells from mouse embryonic fibroblasts more efficiently than other factor combinations.
results.explanation: While there is evidence that the SNEL combination (Sall4, Nanog, Esrrb, Lin28) can successfully reprogram mouse embryonic fibroblasts into iPSCs, the claim of superior efficiency compared to "other factor combinations" is based on extremely limited evidence. Only one primary study (Doc ID 6826100) directly compares SNEL to other combinations including the standard Yamanaka factors, and this comparison uses tetraploid complementation as a measure of "quality" rather than traditional reprogramming efficiency metrics like colony formation rates or conversion percentages. The evidence base lacks independent replication studies and quantitative efficiency data that would be necessary to support such a broad comparative claim. Additionally, the study focuses on developmental potential rather than reprogramming efficiency per se, making the interpretation of "more efficiently" ambiguous.

evidence.passage: Based on tetraploid complementation, we found that ectopic expression of Sall4, Nanog, Esrrb, and Lin28 (SNEL) in mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs) generated high-quality iPSCs more efficiently than other combinations of factors including OSKM
Expression of Esrrb, Utf1, Lin28, and Dppa2 is a better predictor for cells to progress into iPSCs than expression of the previously suggested reprogramming markers Fbxo15, Fgf4, and Oct4
Somatic cells can be induced into pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) with a combination of four transcription factors, Oct4/Sox2/Klf4/c-Myc or Oct4/Sox2/Nanog/LIN28

results.claim: Bcp1 is a chaperone for Rpl23.
results.explanation: The claim that "Bcp1 is a chaperone for Rpl23" is unsupported due to a complete absence of evidence mentioning Bcp1 protein in any context across all reviewed sources. While the evidence demonstrates that ribosomal proteins do require chaperone assistance during assembly (as shown with Yar1 chaperoning RpS3 in Doc 15893330), and establishes that ribosomal proteins can form specific interactions (such as B23-RPS9 in Doc 26495128 and L23-HDM2 in Doc 2582169), none of the evidence establishes the existence of Bcp1 or any relationship between Bcp1 and Rpl23. The low relevance scores (0.43-0.60) across all evidence passages further confirm the lack of supporting material for this specific claim.

evidence.passage: Eukaryotic ribosomes assemble by association of ribosomal RNA with ribosomal proteins into nuclear precursor particles, which undergo a complex maturation pathway coordinated by non-ribosomal assembly factors. Here, we provide functional insights into how successive structural re-arrangements in ribosomal protein S3 promote maturation of the 40S ribosomal subunit. We show that S3 dimerizes and is imported into the nucleus with its N-domain in a rotated conformation and associated with the chaperone Yar1.

B23 (NPM/nucleophosmin) is a multifunctional nucleolar protein and a member of the nucleoplasmin superfamily of acidic histone chaperones. B23 is essential for normal embryonic development and plays an important role in genomic stability, ribosome biogenesis, and anti-apoptotic signaling. Although described as multifunctional, a genuine molecular function of B23 is not fully understood. Here we show that B23 is associated with a protein complex consisting of ribosomal proteins and ribosome-associated RNA helicases. A novel, RNA-independent interaction between ribosomal protein S9 (RPS9) and B23 was further investigated

The importance of coordinating cell growth with proliferation has been recognized for a long time. The molecular basis of this relationship, however, is poorly understood. Here we show that the ribosomal protein L23 interacts with HDM2. The interaction involves the central acidic domain of HDM2 and an N-terminal domain of L23.

results.claim: 1/2000 in UK have abnormal PrP positivity.
results.explanation: The claim is well-supported by a large-scale UK study of 32,441 appendix samples that found a prevalence of 493 per million (approximately 1 in 2,030), which is very close to the claimed 1 in 2,000 rate. While the actual measured rate differs slightly from the exact claim, the difference falls within expected statistical variation and the study's confidence interval (282-801 per million) encompasses rates close to 1/2000. The detection methods using PMCA technology demonstrate high reliability with 89% sensitivity and 100% specificity. The study design was robust, involving multiple UK hospitals and representing the best available population-level data for abnormal PrP prevalence in the UK.

evidence.passage: Of the 32,441 appendix samples 16 were positive for abnormal PrP, indicating an overall prevalence of 493 per million population (95% confidence interval 282 to 801 per million)
Irreversibly unlinked and anonymised large scale survey of archived appendix samples from the pathology departments of 41 UK hospitals
serial PMCA enables detection of PrPSc in blood samples of scrapie-afflicted hamsters with 89% sensitivity and 100% specificity

results.claim: miRNAs regulate a unique suite of mRNAs to maintain homeostasis in cells.
results.explanation: While the evidence clearly demonstrates that miRNAs bind to and regulate specific mRNA targets through sequence complementarity (Doc 32324430, 8006440), there is insufficient evidence to support the claim that each miRNA regulates a truly "unique suite" of targets. The available studies lack direct comparative analyses showing distinct, non-overlapping target repertoires between different miRNAs. Additionally, the connection to homeostasis maintenance is largely theoretical rather than experimentally proven - one study even contradicts this, suggesting miRNA target modulation is "unlikely to cause significant effects on gene expression and metabolism" (Doc 18421962). The evidence shows miRNA dysfunction can lead to gene expression dysregulation (Doc 9159495), but this falls short of demonstrating direct homeostasis maintenance mechanisms.

evidence.passage: Post-transcriptional gene regulation (PTGR) of mRNA turnover, localization and translation is mediated by microRNAs (miRNAs) and RNA-binding proteins (RBPs). These regulators exert their effects by binding to specific sequences within their target mRNAs.

Using deep sequencing, we further show that miRNA recycling is limited by target regulation, which promotes posttranscriptional modifications to the 3' end of the miRNA and accelerates the miRNA's rate of decay. These studies provide new insight into the efficiency of miRNA regulation that help to explain how a miRNA can regulate a vast number of transcripts and that identify one of the mechanisms that impart specificity to miRNA decay in mammalian cells.

Furthermore, in response to extreme metabolic liver disease models, global target site abundance of hepatocytes did not change sufficiently to affect miRNA-mediated repression. Thus, modulation of miRNA target abundance is unlikely to cause significant effects on gene expression and metabolism through a ceRNA effect.

Increased ALG-1 abundance in mir-71 loss-of-function mutants led to globally increased miRNA expression. Interestingly, these mutants demonstrated widespread mRNA expression dysregulation and diminished levels of variability both in gene expression and in overall life span.

results.claim: Weighed food records (WFR) result in high completion since they're cheap to run and impose low participant burden.
results.explanation: The available evidence is insufficient to support any of the key claims about weighed food records (WFR). No studies provided data on completion rates, cost-effectiveness, or participant burden of WFR compared to other dietary assessment methods. The evidence consists primarily of validation studies comparing accuracy between different methods rather than examining practical implementation factors. One study (Doc ID: 21993510) actually suggests potential compliance issues, showing significant underreporting in weighed dietary records with average energy intake 20% lower than actual expenditure. Without direct evidence on the claimed benefits of high completion rates, low costs, and reduced participant burden, this claim cannot be evaluated.

evidence.passage: Average recorded energy intakes were significantly lower than measured expenditure in the group overall (9.66 MJ/day v 12.15 MJ/day, 95% confidence interval 1.45 to 3.53 MJ/day). Among those in the upper third of energy intakes the mean (SE) ratio of intake to expenditure was close to 1.0, indicating accurate records (men 1.01 (0.11), women 0.96 (0.08]. In the middle and lower thirds the ratios for men were only 0.74 (0.05) and 0.70 (0.07) respectively and for women 0.89 (0.07) and 0.61 (0.07).

Participants were asked to complete myfood24 and an interviewer-administered MPR on the same day for 2 non-consecutive days at school. Total energy intake (EI) and nutrients recorded by the two methods were compared using intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC), Bland-Altman plots (using between and within-individual information) and weighted κ to assess the agreement.

results.claim: Enhanced early production of inflammatory chemokines improves viral control in the lung.
results.explanation: The claim lacks sufficient direct evidence to support the specific relationship between enhanced early chemokine production and improved viral control in lungs. While one influenza study (19005293) demonstrates that memory CD4+ T cells enhance inflammatory cytokine and chemokine production leading to early viral control, this represents limited evidence for such a broad claim. Most other evidence focuses on general immune mechanisms, interferon signaling, or herpesvirus-encoded receptors rather than host inflammatory chemokine responses. The evidence base consists primarily of single studies with moderate relevance scores and lacks systematic reviews or large-scale studies directly testing the proposed mechanism. More direct experimental evidence specifically examining enhanced early chemokine production and its causal relationship to viral control outcomes is needed to adequately evaluate this claim.

evidence.passage: We found that the response of memory, but not naive, CD4+ T cells enhances production of multiple innate inflammatory cytokines and chemokines (IICs) in the lung and that, during influenza infection, this leads to early control of virus

Our data suggest that the regulation of T cell egress is a mechanism to retain antigen-specific effectors at the site of infection to promote viral clearance, while decreasing the numbers of bystander T cells and preventing overt inflammation
IFN-I blockade before and after establishment of persistent virus infection resulted in enhanced virus clearance and was CD4 T cell-dependent

results.claim: Rapid up-regulation and higher basal expression of interferon-induced genes reduce survival of granule cell neurons that are infected by West Nile virus.
results.explanation: The claim is contradicted by the available evidence. While granule cell neurons do have higher basal expression of interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs) and can be infected by West Nile virus, the evidence shows that these ISGs provide antiviral protection rather than reducing survival. Source 9433958 explicitly states that ISGs "mediated the antiviral effects" and correlate with "enhanced antiviral response" in granule cell neurons, directly contradicting the claim that higher ISG expression reduces survival. Additionally, no direct evidence is provided showing rapid up-regulation of ISGs upon WNV infection or any survival data demonstrating reduced neuronal viability associated with ISG expression. The evidence suggests ISGs are protective mechanisms rather than detrimental factors.

evidence.passage: By transducing cortical neurons with genes that were expressed more highly in granule cell neurons, we identified three interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs; Ifi27, Irg1 and Rsad2 (also known as Viperin)) that mediated the antiviral effects against different neurotropic viruses. Moreover, we found that the epigenetic state and microRNA (miRNA)-mediated regulation of ISGs correlates with enhanced antiviral response in granule cell neurons

The increased susceptibility of IFN-β(-)/(-) mice was accompanied by enhanced viral replication in different tissues. Consistent with a direct role for IFN-β in control of WNV replication, viral titers in ex vivo cultures of macrophages, dendritic cells, fibroblasts, and cerebellar granule cell neurons, but not cortical neurons, from IFN-β(-)/(-) mice were greater than in wild-type cells
Granule cell neurons of the cerebellum and cortical neurons from the cerebral cortex have unique innate immune programs that confer differential susceptibility to viral infection ex vivo and in vivo

results.claim: One in two surgical randomized controlled trials are discontinued early.
results.explanation: The claim that "one in two surgical randomized controlled trials are discontinued early" is directly contradicted by the available evidence. The single study examining this question (Doc ID 6477536) found that only 21% (approximately one in five) of surgical RCTs were discontinued early, not 50% as claimed. This represents a substantial overstatement of the discontinuation rate by more than double the actual observed rate. While the study demonstrates that discontinuation rates in surgical trials have been systematically studied and quantified, the evidence shows that 79% of trials actually proceed to completion, directly refuting the claim that half are discontinued early.

evidence.passage: Of these, 21% (81/395) were discontinued early, most commonly owing to poor recruitment (44%, 36/81). The remaining 314 (79%) trials proceeded to completion
CONCLUSIONS One in five surgical randomised controlled trials are discontinued early, one in three completed trials remain unpublished
OBJECTIVE To determine the rate of early discontinuation and non-publication of randomised controlled trials involving patients undergoing surgery

results.claim: Mutant mice lacking SVCT2 have greatly increased ascorbic acid levels in both brain and adrenals.
results.explanation: The claim is directly contradicted by the available evidence from PMID 19799455. The study clearly demonstrates that SVCT2 knockout mice (Slc23a1−/−) have "undetectable or markedly reduced" ascorbic acid levels in blood and tissues, not increased levels as claimed. These mice show less than 5% of normal ascorbic acid uptake and die within minutes of birth due to severe ascorbic acid deficiency, developing respiratory failure and brain hemorrhage. While the evidence does not provide specific measurements for brain and adrenal tissues, the general finding of severely reduced ascorbic acid across all tissues directly contradicts the claim of "greatly increased" levels. The biological mechanism also supports this contradiction, as SVCT2 is essential for ascorbic acid transport into tissues.

evidence.passage: Cultured embryonic fibroblasts from homozygous Slc23a1−/− mice had less than 5% of normal ascorbic-acid uptake. Ascorbic-acid levels were undetectable or markedly reduced in the blood and tissues of Slc23a1−/− mice.
Slc23a1−/− mice died within a few minutes of birth with respiratory failure and intraparenchymal brain hemorrhage.

We conclude that Slc23a1 is required for transport of ascorbic acid into many tissues and across the placenta. Deficiency of the transporter is lethal in newborn mice, thereby revealing a previously unrecognized requirement for ascorbic acid in the perinatal period.

results.claim: Homozygous deletion of murine Sbds gene from osterix-expressing mesenchymal stem and progenitor cells (MPCs) prevents oxidative stress.
results.explanation: The claim is directly contradicted by available evidence. While Sbds can indeed be deleted from osteoprogenitors (Doc ID 1617327), the evidence shows that Sbds deletion/dysfunction actually increases rather than prevents oxidative stress. Specifically, perturbation of mesenchymal cells in an SDS mouse model "induces mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and activation of DNA damage responses" (Doc ID 3863543), which is the opposite of the claimed protective effect. The evidence demonstrates that mesenchymal cells are susceptible to oxidative stress, but no studies support the claim that Sbds deletion provides protection against oxidative stress in osterix-expressing MPCs.

evidence.passage: Here, we show that perturbation of mesenchymal cells in a mouse model of the pre-leukemic disorder Shwachman-Diamond syndrome (SDS) induces mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and activation of DNA damage responses in hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells.
Deletion of Sbds in mouse osteoprogenitors induced bone marrow dysfunction with myelodysplasia.

Based on these results, we suggest that senescent growth arrest in hMSCs may result from activation of stress signaling pathways and consequent onset of stress responses, due in part to ROS production during prolonged in vitro culture.

results.claim: Aged patients are more susceptible to ischaemia/reperfusion injury.
results.explanation: While the claim has biological plausibility based on age-related physiological changes, the available evidence is insufficient to definitively support that aged patients are more susceptible to ischemia/reperfusion injury. The evidence shows that aging is associated with mitochondrial dysfunction and glutathione deficiency (Doc 39558597), and elderly ICU patients have higher 3-year mortality rates (57% vs 40%, Doc 20606520), but these findings are not specific to I/R injury outcomes. The inflammatory response evidence comes from general aging studies on "inflammaging" (Doc 5035827) rather than I/R-specific contexts. Most critically, there are no direct comparative studies between aged and younger patients specifically examining I/R injury susceptibility or outcomes, making it impossible to draw definitive conclusions about age-related differences in I/R injury susceptibility.

evidence.passage: Aging is associated with impaired fasted oxidation of nonesterified fatty acids (NEFA) suggesting a mitochondrial defect. Aging is also associated with deficiency of glutathione (GSH), an important mitochondrial antioxidant, and with insulin resistance.

The cumulative 3-yr mortality rate among the elderly (57%) was higher (p < .05) than that among the controls (40%). The majority (66%) of the elderly nonsurvivors died within 1 month after intensive care discharge.

Human aging is characterized by a chronic, low-grade inflammation, and this phenomenon has been termed as "inflammaging." Inflammaging is a highly significant risk factor for both morbidity and mortality in the elderly people, as most if not all age-related diseases share an inflammatory pathogenesis.

Ischemia-reperfusion injury is, at least in part, responsible for the morbidity associated with liver surgery under total vascular exclusion or after liver transplantation. The pathophysiology of hepatic ischemia-reperfusion includes a number of mechanisms that contribute to various degrees in the overall injury.

results.claim: Cognitive behavioral therapy is an ineffective treatment for insomnia
results.explanation: The claim that cognitive behavioral therapy is ineffective for insomnia is contradicted by the available evidence. The primary study (Doc ID 5912283) shows CBT-I was superior to the sleep medication zopiclone on 3 out of 4 outcome measures, with participants improving sleep efficiency from 81.4% to 90.1% at 6-month follow-up while the medication group declined. CBT-I participants also spent more time in restorative slow-wave sleep and less time awake during the night, with sustained benefits at 6 months. While the evidence base is limited to one small RCT with 46 participants, the available data directly contradicts rather than supports the claim of ineffectiveness.

evidence.passage: CBT resulted in improved short- and long-term outcomes compared with zopiclone on 3 out of 4 outcome measures. For most outcomes, zopiclone did not differ from placebo. Participants receiving CBT improved their sleep efficiency from 81.4% at pretreatment to 90.1% at 6-month follow-up compared with a decrease from 82.3% to 81.9% in the zopiclone group.

Participants in the CBT group spent much more time in slow-wave sleep (stages 3 and 4) compared with those in other groups, and spent less time awake during the night. Total sleep time was similar in all 3 groups; at 6 months, patients receiving CBT had better sleep efficiency using polysomnography than those taking zopiclone.

A randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trial of 46 adults (mean age, 60.8 y; 22 women) with chronic primary insomnia conducted between January 2004 and December 2005 in a single Norwegian university-based outpatient clinic for adults and elderly patients.

results.claim: Increased purity of cytoplasmic membranes isolated from overexpressors is indicated by stronger spots for OmpF in 2D BN-PAGE gels.
results.explanation: This claim is fundamentally flawed and contradicts established membrane biology principles. OmpF is an outer membrane protein, not a cytoplasmic membrane protein, so its presence would actually indicate contamination rather than purity in cytoplasmic membrane preparations. The evidence review reveals no direct support for any of the sub-claims: there is no evidence that 2D BN-PAGE can effectively detect OmpF, no demonstration that overexpression systems produce higher purity cytoplasmic membranes, and no establishment of the correlation between spot intensity and membrane purity. All provided evidence passages have low relevance scores and appear tangentially related at best, discussing general membrane biology topics rather than the specific methodological claims made.
